In 1585, Hideyasu was granted {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle}} in {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}} with a stipend of 30,000 koku. == History as Daimyo == The Koide family continued to serve under the Toyotomi and later the Tokugawa regimes. Hideyasu's son, [[Koide Yoshimasa]], was granted {{ill|Tatsuno Castle|ja|龍野城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuno Castle|1=Tatsuno Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856352}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province}}. By 1595, the family was relocated to {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|WD=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain}}, receiving 60,000 koku. Despite siding with the Western Army during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they avoided confiscation of their domain thanks to Yoshimasa's younger brother, who fought for the Eastern Army. Over time, the Koide family's holdings were redistributed, and different branches governed {{ill|Kishiwada Domain|en|Kishiwada Domain|WD=Q3034469|lt=Kishiwada Domain}} and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}}. The Sonobe branch persisted until the Meiji Restoration. == Koide Clan as Kazoku == After the Meiji Restoration, the head of the Sonobe branch, [[Koide Hidenao]], was appointed governor of Sonobe Domain. In 1884, his descendant [[Koide Hidefumi]] was ennobled as a Viscount under the Kazoku system. The family maintained their status as part of the Japanese nobility into the early 20th century. == Family Crest == The Koide clan used the crests "Maruni-Gaku" (circle with a framed pattern) and "Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji" (framed pattern with the characters for two and eight), along with an alternative crest, "Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji" (hexagon with the character for 'Ko'). == Notable Members == * [[Koide Hideyasu]] – First daimyo of Kishiwada Castle * [[Koide Yoshimasa]] – Daimyo of Tatsuno and Izushi * [[Koide Hidenao]] – Last daimyo of Sonobe Domain == References == * {{Cite book|author=Ota Ryo|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kanko Kai|year=1934}} * {{Cite book|author=Kanzan Nitta|title=Nihon Jinbutsu Shiso|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1984}} * {{Cite book|author=Yuji Odabe|title=Kazoku no Jitsuzo|publisher=Chuko Shinsho|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|author=Masaomi Asami|title=Kizoku Tanjyo|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} == See Also == * [[Japanese clans]] * {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle}} * {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|小出氏|version=101122139|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Koide (Klan)]] [[ja:小出氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11459112}} [[Category:Clans of Chūbu Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Ina, Nagano]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Kamiina District]] [[Category:Koide Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Shinano Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[de:Koide (Klan)]][[ja:小出氏]] [[de:Koide (Klan)]][[ja:小出氏]] [[de:Koide (Klan)]][[ja:小出氏]] {{Citation needed|date=May 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Name = Koide Clan (小出氏) | Mon = [[File:Mon Gaku.jpg|50px]] [[File:Mon Gaku 2-8.jpg|50px]] | Mon description = Maruni-Gaku, Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji, Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji | Founder = [[Koide Tokiuji]] (Nikaido lineage) or [[Koide Yoshitsuna]] (Kudo lineage) | Home Province = {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province}}, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}} | Prominent members = [[Koide Hideyasu]]
[[Koide Yoshimasa]]
[[Koide Hideie]]
[[Koide Yoshiie]]
[[Koide Yoshichika]]
[[Koide Hidenao]] | Ruled until = Abolition of the han system in 1871 | Titles = Daimyo, Viscount (Kazoku) | Dissolution = | Cadet Branches = Lords of {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|WD=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain}}, {{ill|Toki Domain|ja|陶器藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Domain|1=Tōki Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10863472}}, and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} }} '''Koide Clan''' (小出氏) was a Japanese samurai and noble family. The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, becoming a daimyo family. They ruled the {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} until the abolition of the feudal domain system during the [[Meiji Restoration]], after which they were ennobled as Viscounts in the [[Kazoku]] peerage system. == Origins == The Koide Clan claims descent from the [[:en:Nikaido clan|Nikaido clan]] of the [[Southern Fujiwara]] lineage, tracing their roots to {{ill|Koide Village|ja|小出村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koide Village|1=koidemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459106}} in the {{ill|Kamiina District, Nagano|en|Kamiina District, Nagano|WD=Q1155054|lt=Ina District, Nagano}} of {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}. According to the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}, members of the clan served in various roles for the Kamakura shogunate, including ceremonial horse-handlers. Other sources, such as the {{ill|Hyakka Keizu Ko|ja|百家系図稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakka Keizu Ko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, suggest a connection to the {{ill|Kudo clan|ja|工藤氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudo clan|1=Kudō clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479597}}. The clan's prominence began with [[Koide Hideyasu]], who became the husband of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s maternal aunt, securing favor and territory under the Toyotomi regime. In 1585, Hideyasu was granted {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle}} in {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}} with a stipend of 30,000 koku. == History as Daimyo == The Koide family continued to serve under the Toyotomi and later the Tokugawa regimes. Hideyasu's son, [[Koide Yoshimasa]], was granted {{ill|Tatsuno Castle|ja|龍野城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuno Castle|1=Tatsuno Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856352}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province}}. By 1595, the family was relocated to {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|WD=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain}}, receiving 60,000 koku. Despite siding with the Western Army during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they avoided confiscation of their domain thanks to Yoshimasa's younger brother, who fought for the Eastern Army. Over time, the Koide family's holdings were redistributed, and different branches governed {{ill|Kishiwada Domain|en|Kishiwada Domain|WD=Q3034469|lt=Kishiwada Domain}} and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}}. The Sonobe branch persisted until the Meiji Restoration. == Koide Clan as Kazoku == After the Meiji Restoration, the head of the Sonobe branch, [[Koide Hidenao]], was appointed governor of Sonobe Domain. In 1884, his descendant [[Koide Hidefumi]] was ennobled as a Viscount under the Kazoku system. The family maintained their status as part of the Japanese nobility into the early 20th century. == Family Crest == The Koide clan used the crests "Maruni-Gaku" (circle with a framed pattern) and "Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji" (framed pattern with the characters for two and eight), along with an alternative crest, "Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji" (hexagon with the character for 'Ko'). == Notable Members == * [[Koide Hideyasu]] – First daimyo of Kishiwada Castle * [[Koide Yoshimasa]] – Daimyo of Tatsuno and Izushi * [[Koide Hidenao]] – Last daimyo of Sonobe Domain == References == * {{Cite book|author=Ota Ryo|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kanko Kai|year=1934}} * {{Cite book|author=Kanzan Nitta|title=Nihon Jinbutsu Shiso|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1984}} * {{Cite book|author=Yuji Odabe|title=Kazoku no Jitsuzo|publisher=Chuko Shinsho|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|author=Masaomi Asami|title=Kizoku Tanjyo|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} == See Also == * [[Japanese clans]] * {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle}} * {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|小出氏|version=101122139|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Koide (Klan)]] [[ja:小出氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11459112}} [[Category:Clans of Chūbu Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Ina, Nagano]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Kamiina District]] [[Category:Koide Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Shinano Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[Koide Hidenao]] | Ruled until = Abolition of the han system in 1871 | Titles = Daimyo, Viscount (Kazoku) | Dissolution = | Cadet Branches = Lords of {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|WD=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain}}, {{ill|Toki Domain|ja|陶器藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Domain|1=Tōki Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10863472}}, and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} }} '''Koide Clan''' (小出氏) was a Japanese samurai and noble family. The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, becoming a daimyo family. They ruled the {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}} until the abolition of the feudal domain system during the [[Meiji Restoration]], after which they were ennobled as Viscounts in the [[Kazoku]] peerage system. == Origins == The Koide Clan claims descent from the [[:en:Nikaido clan|Nikaido clan]] of the [[Southern Fujiwara]] lineage, tracing their roots to {{ill|Koide Village|ja|小出村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koide Village|1=koidemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459106}} in the {{ill|Kamiina District, Nagano|en|Kamiina District, Nagano|WD=Q1155054|lt=Ina District, Nagano}} of {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}. According to the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}, members of the clan served in various roles for the Kamakura shogunate, including ceremonial horse-handlers. Other sources, such as the {{ill|Hyakka Keizu Ko|ja|百家系図稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakka Keizu Ko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, suggest a connection to the {{ill|Kudo clan|ja|工藤氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudo clan|1=Kudō clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479597}}. The clan's prominence began with [[Koide Hideyasu]], who became the husband of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s maternal aunt, securing favor and territory under the Toyotomi regime. In 1585, Hideyasu was granted {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle}} in {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province}} with a stipend of 30,000 koku. == History as Daimyo == The Koide family continued to serve under the Toyotomi and later the Tokugawa regimes. Hideyasu's son, [[Koide Yoshimasa]], was granted {{ill|Tatsuno Castle|ja|龍野城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuno Castle|1=Tatsuno Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856352}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province}}. By 1595, the family was relocated to {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|WD=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain}}, receiving 60,000 koku. Despite siding with the Western Army during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they avoided confiscation of their domain thanks to Yoshimasa's younger brother, who fought for the Eastern Army. Over time, the Koide family's holdings were redistributed, and different branches governed {{ill|Kishiwada Domain|en|Kishiwada Domain|WD=Q3034469|lt=Kishiwada Domain}} and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}}. The Sonobe branch persisted until the Meiji Restoration. == Koide Clan as Kazoku == After the Meiji Restoration, the head of the Sonobe branch, [[Koide Hidenao]], was appointed governor of Sonobe Domain. In 1884, his descendant [[Koide Hidefumi]] was ennobled as a Viscount under the Kazoku system. The family maintained their status as part of the Japanese nobility into the early 20th century. == Family Crest == The Koide clan used the crests "Maruni-Gaku" (circle with a framed pattern) and "Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji" (framed pattern with the characters for two and eight), along with an alternative crest, "Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji" (hexagon with the character for 'Ko'). == Notable Members == * [[Koide Hideyasu]] – First daimyo of Kishiwada Castle * [[Koide Yoshimasa]] – Daimyo of Tatsuno and Izushi * [[Koide Hidenao]] – Last daimyo of Sonobe Domain == References == * {{Cite book|author=Ota Ryo|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kanko Kai|year=1934}} * {{Cite book|author=Kanzan Nitta|title=Nihon Jinbutsu Shiso|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1984}} * {{Cite book|author=Yuji Odabe|title=Kazoku no Jitsuzo|publisher=Chuko Shinsho|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|author=Masaomi Asami|title=Kizoku Tanjyo|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} == See Also == * [[Japanese clans]] * {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle}} * {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|小出氏|version=101122139|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Koide (Klan)]] [[ja:小出氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11459112}} [[Category:Clans of Chūbu Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Ina, Nagano]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Kamiina District]] [[Category:Koide Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Shinano Province]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

Hideyasu's son, [[Koide Yoshimasa]], was granted {{ill|Tatsuno Castle|ja|龍野城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuno Castle|1=Tatsuno Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856352}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}. Despite siding with the Western Army during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they avoided confiscation of their domain thanks to Yoshimasa's younger brother, who fought for the Eastern Army. Over time, the Koide family's holdings were redistributed, and different branches governed {{ill|Kishiwada Domain|en|Kishiwada Domain|WD=Q3034469|lt=Kishiwada Domain|1=Kishiwada Domain}} and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain|1=Sonobe Domain}}. The Sonobe branch persisted until the Meiji Restoration. == Koide Clan as Kazoku == After the Meiji Restoration, the head of the Sonobe branch, [[Koide Hidenao]], was appointed governor of Sonobe Domain. In 1884, his descendant [[Koide Hidefumi]] was ennobled as a Viscount under the Kazoku system. The family maintained their status as part of the Japanese nobility into the early 20th century. == Family Crest == The Koide clan used the crests "Maruni-Gaku" (circle with a framed pattern) and "Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji" (framed pattern with the characters for two and eight), along with an alternative crest, "Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji" (hexagon with the character for 'Ko'). == Notable Members == * [[Koide Hideyasu]] – First daimyo of Kishiwada Castle * [[Koide Yoshimasa]] – Daimyo of Tatsuno and Izushi * [[Koide Hidenao]] – Last daimyo of Sonobe Domain == References == * {{Cite book|author=Ota Ryo|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kanko Kai|year=1934}} * {{Cite book|author=Kanzan Nitta|title=Nihon Jinbutsu Shiso|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1984}} * {{Cite book|author=Yuji Odabe|title=Kazoku no Jitsuzo|publisher=Chuko Shinsho|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|author=Masaomi Asami|title=Kizoku Tanjyo|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} == See Also == * [[Japanese clans]] * {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|WD=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle|1=Kishiwada Castle}} * {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|WD=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain|1=Sonobe Domain}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|小出氏|version=101122139|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Koide (Klan)]] [[ja:小出氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11459112}} [[Category:Clans of Chūbu Region]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Ina, Nagano]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Kamiina District]] [[Category:Koide Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Shinano Province]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[de:Koide (Klan)]][[ja:小出氏]] [[de:Koide (Klan)]][[ja:小出氏]] [[de:Koide (Klan)]][[ja:小出氏]] {{Citation needed|date=May 2017}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | Name = Koide Clan (小出氏) | Mon = [[File:Mon Gaku.jpg|50px]] [[File:Mon Gaku 2-8.jpg|50px]] | Mon description = Maruni-Gaku, Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji, Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji | Founder = [[Koide Tokiuji]] (Nikaido lineage) or [[Koide Yoshitsuna]] (Kudo lineage) | Home Province = {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|qid=Q666514|lt=Echigo Province|1=Echigo Province}}, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} | Prominent members = [[Koide Hideyasu]]
[[Koide Yoshimasa]]
[[Koide Hideie]]
[[Koide Yoshiie]]
[[Koide Yoshichika]]
[[Koide Hidenao]] | Ruled until = Abolition of the han system in 1871 | Titles = Daimyo, Viscount (Kazoku) | Dissolution = | Cadet Branches = Lords of {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|qid=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain|1=Izushi Domain}}, {{ill|Toki Domain|ja|陶器藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Domain|1=Tōki Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10863472}}, and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|qid=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain|1=Sonobe Domain}} }} '''Koide Clan''' (小出氏) was a Japanese samurai and noble family. The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, becoming a daimyo family. They ruled the {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|qid=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain|1=Sonobe Domain}} in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} until the abolition of the feudal domain system during the [[Meiji Restoration]], after which they were ennobled as Viscounts in the [[Kazoku]] peerage system. == Origins == The Koide Clan claims descent from the [[:en:Nikaido clan|Nikaido clan]] of the [[Southern Fujiwara]] lineage, tracing their roots to {{ill|Koide Village|ja|小出村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koide Village|1=koidemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11459106}} in the {{ill|Kamiina District, Nagano|en|Kamiina District, Nagano|qid=Q1155054|lt=Ina District, Nagano|1=Kamiina District, Nagano}} of {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}. According to the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|qid=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}, members of the clan served in various roles for the Kamakura shogunate, including ceremonial horse-handlers. Other sources, such as the {{ill|Hyakka Keizu Ko|ja|百家系図稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakka Keizu Ko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, suggest a connection to the {{ill|Kudo clan|ja|工藤氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kudo clan|1=Kudō clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11479597}}. The clan's prominence began with [[Koide Hideyasu]], who became the husband of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s maternal aunt, securing favor and territory under the Toyotomi regime. In 1585, Hideyasu was granted {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|qid=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle|1=Kishiwada Castle}} in {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|qid=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}} with a stipend of 30,000 koku. == History as Daimyo == The Koide family continued to serve under the Toyotomi and later the Tokugawa regimes. Hideyasu's son, [[Koide Yoshimasa]], was granted {{ill|Tatsuno Castle|ja|龍野城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuno Castle|1=Tatsuno Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10856352}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}. By 1595, the family was relocated to {{ill|Izushi Domain|en|Izushi Domain|qid=Q10863249|lt=Izushi Domain|1=Izushi Domain}}, receiving 60,000 koku. Despite siding with the Western Army during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, they avoided confiscation of their domain thanks to Yoshimasa's younger brother, who fought for the Eastern Army. Over time, the Koide family's holdings were redistributed, and different branches governed {{ill|Kishiwada Domain|en|Kishiwada Domain|qid=Q3034469|lt=Kishiwada Domain|1=Kishiwada Domain}} and {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|qid=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain|1=Sonobe Domain}}. The Sonobe branch persisted until the Meiji Restoration. == Koide Clan as Kazoku == After the Meiji Restoration, the head of the Sonobe branch, [[Koide Hidenao]], was appointed governor of Sonobe Domain. In 1884, his descendant [[Koide Hidefumi]] was ennobled as a Viscount under the Kazoku system. The family maintained their status as part of the Japanese nobility into the early 20th century. == Family Crest == The Koide clan used the crests "Maruni-Gaku" (circle with a framed pattern) and "Gaku-ni-Nihachi-moji" (framed pattern with the characters for two and eight), along with an alternative crest, "Kikko-ni-Ko-no-ji" (hexagon with the character for 'Ko'). == Notable Members == * [[Koide Hideyasu]] – First daimyo of Kishiwada Castle * [[Koide Yoshimasa]] – Daimyo of Tatsuno and Izushi * [[Koide Hidenao]] – Last daimyo of Sonobe Domain == References == * {{Cite book|author=Ota Ryo|title=Seishi Kakei Daijiten|publisher=Seishi Kakei Daijiten Kanko Kai|year=1934}} * {{Cite book|author=Kanzan Nitta|title=Nihon Jinbutsu Shiso|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1984}} * {{Cite book|author=Yuji Odabe|title=Kazoku no Jitsuzo|publisher=Chuko Shinsho|year=2006}} * {{Cite book|author=Masaomi Asami|title=Kizoku Tanjyo|publisher=Libroport|year=1994}} == See Also == * [[Japanese clans]] * {{ill|Kishiwada Castle|en|Kishiwada Castle|qid=Q2969493|lt=Kishiwada Castle|1=Kishiwada Castle}} * {{ill|Sonobe Domain|en|Sonobe Domain|qid=Q10863377|lt=Sonobe Domain|1=Sonobe Domain}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{translated page|ja|小出氏|version=101122139|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Koide (Klan)]] [[ja:小出氏]] {{wikidata link|Q11459112}} [[Category:Clans of Chūbu Region]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Ina, Nagano]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Kamiina District]] [[Category:Koide Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Shinano Province]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
